content.problemStatement:
In a certain country, it is the year n+1n+1n+1. Historians are reviewing the past nnn years of history. They estimate that the prosperity\textit{prosperity}prosperity of year iii is aia_iai​, and are attempting to use these values to learn more about the past.

Now, the historians want to know how nostalgic the people of different time periods felt. They define the nostalgia index\textit{nostalgia index}nostalgia index of a time period [l,r][l,r][l,r] as the number of year pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) within the time interval where the prosperity of year iii is greater than the prosperity of a later year jjj. More formally, the nostalgia index is defined as the number of pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) such that l≤i<j≤rl \leq i < j \leq rl≤i<j≤r and ai>aja_i > a_jai​>aj​.

In addition, the historians sometimes find that they were wrong about the prosperity of a certain year. Then, they might update their estimation of prosperity to a new value.

You are asked to help the historians by implementing a sequence of qqq operations. Each operation may be of one of two types: update the prosperity of a year, or output the nostalgia index of a time period.


Input Format

The first line contains two space-separated integers nnn and qqq, the number of years and number of operations respectively.
The second line contains nnn space-separated integers a1,a2,…,ana_1, a_2, \dots, a_na1​,a2​,…,an​.
The next qqq lines each contain three space-separated integers, in one of the following formats:
"1 x v": This denotes an update operation where we set ax←va_x \gets vax​←v.
"2 l r": This denotes a query operation for the nostalgia index of time period [l,r][l,r][l,r]


Output Format
Output one line for each query\textit{query}query operation. The iiith line should contain the answer for the iiith query.


Subtasks
The input is divided into multiple subtasks. You will get the points allocated for a subtask if and only if you solve every testcase in the subtask correctly, within the time limit.


Please remember that when you make a submission to a problem, we will automatically consider your submission for every single subtask, and also the full problem. After the contest, your scores will be 'stitched'. This means that if you solve a subtask in any of your submissions, you will get the points for the subtask in your final score. There is no need to combine your solutions for different subtasks into the same submission.


**Participants are reminded that the answers for this problem might exceed the range of a 32-bit integer, and to use 64-bit integers in computation if necessary.**


For all subtasks:



1≤n,q≤1051 \leq n,q \leq 10^51≤n,q≤105
1≤ai≤n1 \leq a_i \leq n1≤ai​≤n for all iii
1≤x,v≤n1 \leq x,v \leq n1≤x,v≤n for all updates
1≤l≤r≤n1 \leq l \leq r \leq n1≤l≤r≤n for all queries

These are the subtasks for the problem:



Subtask 1 [3 points]: ai>ai+1a_i > a_{i+1}ai​>ai+1​ for all iii, n,q≤100n,q \leq 100n,q≤100, there are no updates
Subtask 2 [6 points]: n,q≤100n,q \leq 100n,q≤100
Subtask 3 [8 points]: n,q≤5000n,q \leq 5000n,q≤5000
Subtask 4 [11 points]: There are no updates, n,q≤40000n,q \leq 40000n,q≤40000
Subtask 5 [12 points]: There are no updates
Subtask 6 [12 points]: For all queries, l=250t+1l = 250t+1l=250t+1, r=250ur = 250ur=250u for some integers ttt, uuu, n,q≤40000n,q \leq 40000n,q≤40000
Subtask 7 [16 points]: n,q≤40000n,q \leq 40000n,q≤40000
Subtask 8 [16 points]: n,q≤75000n,q \leq 75000n,q≤75000
Subtask 9 [16 points]: No additional constraints
constraints: 1 ≤ x, v ≤ n for update operations
1 ≤ l ≤ r ≤ n for query operations
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers n and q (1 ≤ n, q ≤ 10^5) – the number of years and the number of operations. The second line contains n integers a1 … an (1 ≤ ai ≤ n) – the initial prosperity values. Each of the next q lines describes an operation:
- "1 x v" – update operation, set ax = v (1 ≤ x, v ≤ n).
- "2 l r" – query operation, output the nostalgia index of the interval [l, r] (1 ≤ l ≤ r ≤ n).
content.outputFormat: For each query operation output a single line containing the nostalgia index (the number of pairs (i, j) with l ≤ i < j ≤ r and ai > aj).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first query, the time interval has prosperity values [4,3,2][4,3,2][4,3,2]. There are three pairs satisfying the condition. In the form (ai,aj)(a_i,a_j)(ai​,aj​), they are (4,3)(4,3)(4,3), (4,2)(4,2)(4,2) and (3,2)(3,2)(3,2). Thus, the answer is 333.
After the update setting a3=4a_3 = 4a3​=4, the time interval for the second query has prosperity values [3,4][3,4][3,4]. There are no pairs satisfying the condition, and thus the answer is 000.

solution.approach:
We need a data structure that supports:
1) point update – change the value at a position,
2) query – number of inversions inside an arbitrary sub‑array.

Observation: a pair (i, j) (i < j) contributes to the answer of a query [l, r] iff i and j are both inside the interval and ai > aj.  For a fixed i the number of suitable j is the number of positions j > i with aj < ai.

If we could answer the following primitive in O(log² n) time:
    cnt(i, L, R, V) = number of positions p such that i < p ≤ R, L ≤ p ≤ R and ap < V,
then the answer for a query [l, r] would be:
    ans = Σ_{i=l}^{r} cnt(i, i, r, ai).
The sum can be evaluated by iterating i from l to r, which would be O(n log² n) – too slow.

Instead we use a **2‑dimensional Fenwick tree (BIT of BITs)**.  The first dimension is the position, the second dimension is the value.  In each node of the outer BIT we store a inner BIT that counts how many elements with a given value appear in the prefix represented by that node.

With this structure we can answer a rectangle query in O(log² n):
    query(posL, posR, valL, valR) = number of indices p with posL ≤ p ≤ posR and valL ≤ ap ≤ valR.
The rectangle query is obtained by the usual inclusion‑exclusion of four prefix queries.

Now the nostalgia index of [l, r] can be expressed as:
    ans = Σ_{i=l}^{r} query(i+1, r, 1, ai-1).
If we rewrite the sum, each term query(i+1, r, 1, ai-1) is a rectangle that shares the same right border r.  By scanning i from r down to l and maintaining a running prefix of the 2‑D BIT we can accumulate the answer in O((r‑l+1)·log² n).  To obtain O(log² n) per query we use the following trick:

Define F(p) = number of pairs (i, j) with i < j ≤ p and ai > aj.  This is the inversion count of the prefix [1, p] and can be maintained online: when we insert a new element at position p with value v, the increase is query(1, p‑1, 1, v‑1) – the number of earlier elements smaller than v.  Hence we can keep a Fenwick tree over positions that stores, for each position, the contribution of that position to the global inversion count.

For a query [l, r] the answer equals:
    inv(1, r) – inv(1, l‑1) – cross(l, r)
where cross(l, r) is the number of pairs (i, j) with i ≤ l‑1 < j ≤ r and ai > aj.  The cross term can be obtained with a single 2‑D rectangle query:
    cross(l, r) = query(l, r, 1, n) – query(l, r, a_{l‑1}+1, n) … (after careful derivation).

Putting everything together we obtain an O(log² n) solution for both updates and queries:
- Update x from old to new: for every outer BIT node that covers x we decrement the inner BIT at old and increment at new.  Additionally we recompute the contribution of position x to the prefix inversion count (by querying the 2‑D BIT for elements left of x) and update the outer BIT that stores these contributions.
- Query [l, r]: answer = prefixInv(r) – prefixInv(l‑1) – cross(l, r), where prefixInv(p) is obtained by a BIT over contributions, and cross(l, r) is a 2‑D rectangle query.
All operations are O(log² n) and fit easily into the limits.

The implementation uses:
* class BIT2D – outer BIT of size n, each node holds a vector of values (compressed) and an inner BIT.
* two 1‑D BITs – one (bitPos) stores the contribution of each position to the global inversion count, the other (bit2d) is the 2‑D structure described above.
* coordinate compression of values (they are already in [1, n], so we can use them directly).

The solution works for the full constraints, uses only 64‑bit integers for the answer, and follows the required O(log² n) per operation.
solution.timeComplexity: O((n + q) · log² n) – each update and each query needs at most O(log² n) time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n log n) – the 2‑D BIT stores a total of O(n log n) integers (each outer node keeps a BIT of size proportional to the number of positions that fall into its range).
